dates.title: AI 把手伸进流程前，企业先买的是“接入和治理”
dates.dek:
过去一年，AI 编程工具的卖点几乎都围绕“它更会写”。但在企业里，真正让人犹豫的往往不是它能不能写出一段像样的代码，而是下一步：它能不能被允许去读完整的代码库，能不能去调用内部工具，能不能触碰数据库，能不能在出错后留下足够清楚的记录，能不能在被误用时立刻停下来。

当 AI 开始从“给答案”走向“替你做事”，它就不再只是一个更聪明的界面，而更像一个新的执行者。执行者越强，组织越会先问三件事：它能碰什么，错了谁负责，怎么证明它做对了。

dates.title: AI 编程进企业，第一道门不是代码，是授权
dates.dek:
企业不会因为 AI 更会写代码，就立刻让它进入真实系统。

真正让人犹豫的是下一步：能不能让它读完整代码库，能不能调用内部工具，能不能碰数据库，能不能提交修改，能不能在出错后留下足够清楚的记录。AI 编程越接近真实工作，这些问题越不再是安全部门的补充条款，而是采购前就要问清楚的条件。

dates.title: AI 进了工作流，控制权和风险先浮出水面
dates.dek:
今天的 AI 新闻没有一个漂亮的中心。没有那种一看就能上热搜的模型发布，也没有一句能被到处转发的宏大口号。

但几条材料摆在一起，味道很清楚：AI 正在从“会回答”往“会动手”挪。它一旦动手，就会碰产品组织、代码仓库、数据库、金融风控和供应商选择。热闹少了一点，生意味反而更重。

points.title: Aaron Levie｜One of the best things students and colleges can do is not bail on learning and teaching t
points.originalView: One of the best things students and colleges can do is not bail on learning and teaching the fundamentals of any given domain. AI will trick you into thinking you don’t need to go deep in a particular area, but that’s wrong. The expert with AI is always going to be far more capable than the novice. Those that can steer AI agents properly, figure out how to evaluate their work, fix their mistakes, and incorporate their work into a workflow will always be the most potent users of these tools. The experienced software developer that’s built and scaled complex systems using agents outrun someone just vibe coding. The designer that uses AI will build far better products and campaigns than anyone else. The banker or analyst that understands financial models will be able to pull off far more with agents. Despite some of the rhetoric in the valley that this is less implement now, that couldn’t be further from the case. Don’t give up on going deep in your craft. ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI likes=425; retweets=53; replies=41

points.title: Nikunj Kothari｜Employees joining some recently minted unicorn* companies are facing a triple whammy.. 1)
points.originalView: Employees joining some recently minted unicorn* companies are facing a triple whammy.. 1) tranched valuations are very common now means their entry strike price at that valuation is often >2x of the lead preferred price. 2) this means your strike price will be quite high and so will be your 409a. you’ll paying a pretty penny if you try to early exercise and if not then you’ll have to wait a while till they grow into that valuation. 3) stock packages seem great “on paper” but are massively inflated because of this tranched valuation. so you can say total comp is very high but the real comp is not that high (at least today). this is another reason companies chase a higher valuation so they can give lower equity raises. Make sure you go with wide eyes and use Claude/ChatGPT to do some exit math on your equity. Joining a unicorn company can offer far more than just comp (learning, experience, strong mission, great team) and that should be the core reason for joining. But do your own due diligence on the company you’re joining just like an investor would. * Not all unicorn companies are the same - some are fundamentally better and have meaningfully grown into the valuation than others. Some are growing way faster than the ones with fundamentals. Point is to do your homework vs treating every company that’s valued similarly and putting them in the same bucket. partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧 likes=123; retweets=5; replies=8

points.title: Aaron Levie｜Right now there’s a temporary mismatch between the jobs that used to be sought after in so
points.originalView: Right now there’s a temporary mismatch between the jobs that used to be sought after in some fields and the new jobs that are becoming in demand in those fields. For instance, if you studied CS, for years the general direction of travel was often to join a tech company and build customer-facing software in some form. A significant portion of the CS pipeline from college to hire was built for this. When you realize that AI is going to make coding abundant, you realize everyone will need technical talent to implement agentic systems. This means the types of roles engineers should be thinking about radically expands. I was talking to a Fortune 500 pharma CEO a week ago that commented on how much more technical talent they need right now. The job may be different from what it was 5 years ago when thinking about tech, but the demand for the skills are still there. And this is what I’m hearing from every CIO and CEO across nearly every industry right now. We definitely need colleges to wake up to this; but we equally need companies think about how they craft pipelines into these jobs. ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI likes=275; retweets=24; replies=37

The experienced software developer that’s built and scaled complex systems using agents outrun someone just vibe coding. The designer that uses AI will build far better products and campaigns than anyone else. The banker or analyst that understands financial models will be able to pull off far more with agents. Despite some of the rhetoric in the valley that this is less implement now, that couldn’t be further from the case. points.title: Nikunj Kothari｜Employees joining some recently minted unicorn* companies are facing a triple whammy.. 1)
points.originalView: Employees joining some recently minted unicorn* companies are facing a triple whammy.. 1) tranched valuations are very common now means their entry strike price at that valuation is often >2x of the lead preferred price. 2) this means your strike price will be quite high and so will be your 409a. you’ll paying a pretty penny if you try to early exercise and if not then you’ll have to wait a while till they grow into that valuation. 3) stock packages seem great “on paper” but are massively inflated because of this tranched valuation. so you can say total comp is very high but the real comp is not that high (at least today). this is another reason companies chase a higher valuation so they can give lower equity raises. Make sure you go with wide eyes and use Claude/ChatGPT to do some exit math on your equity. Joining a unicorn company can offer far more than just comp (learning, experience, strong mission, great team) and that should be the core reason for joining. But do your own due diligence on the company you’re joining just like an investor would. * Not all unicorn companies are the same - some are fundamentally better and have meaningfully grown into the valuation than others. Some are growing way faster than the ones with fundamentals. Point is to do your homework vs treating every company that’s valued similarly and putting them in the same bucket. partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧 likes=123; retweets=5; replies=8

points.title: Aaron Levie｜Right now there’s a temporary mismatch between the jobs that used to be sought after in so
points.originalView: Right now there’s a temporary mismatch between the jobs that used to be sought after in some fields and the new jobs that are becoming in demand in those fields. For instance, if you studied CS, for years the general direction of travel was often to join a tech company and build customer-facing software in some form. A significant portion of the CS pipeline from college to hire was built for this. When you realize that AI is going to make coding abundant, you realize everyone will need technical talent to implement agentic systems. This means the types of roles engineers should be thinking about radically expands. I was talking to a Fortune 500 pharma CEO a week ago that commented on how much more technical talent they need right now. The job may be different from what it was 5 years ago when thinking about tech, but the demand for the skills are still there. And this is what I’m hearing from every CIO and CEO across nearly every industry right now. We definitely need colleges to wake up to this; but we equally need companies think about how they craft pipelines into these jobs. ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI likes=275; retweets=24; replies=37

daily.title: AI 把手伸进流程前，企业先买的是“接入和治理”
daily.judgment: 过去一年，AI 编程工具的卖点几乎都围绕“它更会写”。但在企业里，真正让人犹豫的往往不是它能不能写出一段像样的代码，而是下一步：它能不能被允许去读完整的代码库，能不能去调用内部工具，能不能触碰数据库，能不能在出错后留下足够清楚的记录，能不能在被误用时立刻停下来。
daily.dek: 过去一年，AI 编程工具的卖点几乎都围绕“它更会写”。但在企业里，真正让人犹豫的往往不是它能不能写出一段像样的代码，而是下一步：它能不能被允许去读完整的代码库，能不能去调用内部工具，能不能触碰数据库，能不能在出错后留下足够清楚的记录，能不能在被误用时立刻停下来。
summary: 过去一年，AI 编程工具的卖点几乎都围绕“它更会写”。但在企业里，真正让人犹豫的往往不是它能不能写出一段像样的代码，而是下一步：它能不能被允许去读完整的代码库，能不能去调用内部工具，能不能触碰数据库，能不能在出错后留下足够清楚的记录，能不能在被误用时立刻停下来。
当 AI 开始从“给答案”走向“替你做事”，它就不再只是一个更聪明的界面，而更像一个新的执行者。执行者越强，组织越会先问三件事：它能碰什么，错了谁负责，怎么证明它做对了。
5 月 19 日的材料里，这条脉络变得更清楚：平台公司在加固“接入层”，开源社区在堆“能跑起来的 Agent”，安全团队在补“能控住的执行方式”，而公众与监管的态度提醒企业——即便工具更强，信任也不会自动到账。

sections.title: 连接能力正在从“工程细节”变成“平台主线”
sections.body:
Anthropic 宣布收购 Stainless（官方公告链接：https://www.anthropic.com/news/anthropic-acquires-stainless），这类动作值得看，不是因为“又一笔收购”，而是因为它把一个长期被当成工程附件的东西推到了台前：SDK、CLI、连接器与工具调用的基础设施。

公告里有一句非常直白的话：AI 的前沿正在从“回答问题的模型”转向“能采取行动的 Agent”，而 Agent 的能力取决于它能连接到什么系统、以什么方式连接。把 SDK 与 MCP server tooling 作为平台能力去做，等于在提前回答企业的疑问：当一个模型要进入真实流程时，它通过什么接口触达系统、谁来维护这些接口、如何让跨语言与跨团队的接入变得可控。

这背后是一种很现实的预算迁移：从“买一个更强的模型/插件”，到“买一整套能让它进入流程的连接与管控”。对企业来说，接入越顺滑，风险也越需要被显式管理；否则“能调用一切”的能力，等价于“难以追责”的风险。

sections.title: 开源世界在加速“让 Agent 跑起来”，企业会追问“怎么关得住”
sections.body:
同一天的开发者平台环境里，另一个信号来自开源项目：越来越多项目不满足于做聊天式助手，而是直接把 Agent 往可执行的方向推。

例如 Airlock（GitHub：https://github.com/airlockrun/airlock/）和 Skyvern（GitHub：https://github.com/Skyvern-AI/skyvern）都把重点放在“让 Agent 去做具体动作”：自我升级、浏览器自动化、跨工具执行。它们未必代表成熟产品，但它们能解释企业接下来会买什么——当执行变得容易，“怎么限制执行”会更值钱。

工程组织真正害怕的不是 AI 写错一行代码，而是它在拥有权限后做出一串连锁动作：开了一个端口、换了一个依赖、改了一个策略、写入了错误数据，最后留下一个难以复盘的现场。越接近生产环境，越需要沙箱、审计、回滚、成本监控和明确的“必须人工确认”的边界。

sections.title: 安全团队已经在用同一套逻辑：不是让模型更聪明，而是给它套上“执行框架”
sections.body:
Cloudflare 的一篇文章（https://blog.cloudflare.com/cyber-frontier-models）提供了一个很典型的对照：他们尝试把前沿模型用于漏洞挖掘，结果发现问题不在“模型够不够强”，而在“单个 Agent 的交互形态不适合覆盖真实代码库”。他们把重心转向一个“harness”式的执行框架：把任务拆小、并行跑、加入对抗式复核、把不同问题拆开问、做结果去重。

这其实是在把 Agent 当成一个需要被组织起来的执行系统，而不是一个会聊天的工具。安全场景之所以先出现这种框架，不难理解：这里的失败代价更高，误报和漏报都会浪费大量人力。可一旦你把这种框架抽象出来，它会自然外溢到更多企业流程里——从代码，到工单，到客服，到合规审阅，所有“可复盘的工作”都会要求同样的能力：把执行拆成可控的步骤，把每一步留痕，把结果放进可验证的队列里。

换句话说，市场正在把“Agent 的能力”拆成两部分卖：一部分是模型本身的推理能力，另一部分是让它能被部署、被授权、被追责的执行系统。后者往往更接近企业采购。

sections.title: 信任与监管的现实，会把“治理”从选配变成必答题
sections.body:
当执行能力变强，组织会更谨慎地对待“把权交出去”。一方面是安全与合规的硬约束，另一方面是社会层面的信任问题。

The Verge 报道了一项由皮尤研究中心与盖洛普相关调查引出的结论：多数美国受访者对 AI 以及负责管理它的人缺乏信任（文章链接：https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/644853/pew-gallup-data-americans-dont-trust-ai）。这类情绪并不直接决定企业采购，但它会通过更具体的方式回到企业：客户更敏感、员工更警惕、监管更愿意介入、董事会更在意风险解释。

同一天，The Decoder 也报道了美国政治力量对“前沿模型监管”的讨论（https://the-decoder.com/maga-aligned-groups-want-government-oversight-of-frontier-ai-models）。不论你是否同意它的立场，一个事实是：当 AI 影响更大的决策与执行，治理会越来越早出现在产品问题清单里，而不是上线后的补丁。

这会改变企业对“先进能力”的容忍度。能力可以更激进，但必须能说清：权限怎么给、数据怎么隔离、日志怎么留、错误怎么回滚、成本怎么封顶、违规怎么追责。没有这套机制，再强的演示也更难进入正式预算。

sections.title: 这条主线今天为什么更清楚
sections.body:
如果把 5 月 19 日看到的动作放在一起，它们并不是在证明“企业已经大规模买单”，而是在解释一件更务实的事：AI 的竞争焦点正在从“它会不会写”转向“组织敢不敢让它做”。

Aaron Levie 在 X 上的一个观点很贴近这种岗位变化：当 coding 变得更充裕，企业反而更需要技术人才去把 agentic systems 放进真实组织（来源：https://x.com/levie/status/2056219645796090197）。这不是“工程师不重要了”，而是“工程师的价值会向边界、评估与治理移动”。你需要能定义任务、设计验证、控制权限、处理异常的人，来把 AI 的执行变成可交付、可复盘、可追责的结果。

因此，今天更值得关注的不是某个单点工具，而是下一类会真正拿到企业预算的东西：接入层（SDK、连接器、工具协议）、执行框架（拆解、并发、复核、去重、验收）、治理系统（权限、审计、沙箱、回滚、成本封顶、合规控制）。

接下来更关键的观察点也会更具体：哪家平台把连接能力做成了可被安全团队接受的标准配置？哪类产品开始公开定价、出现可复用的部署案例？哪些组织愿意把“允许自动执行”的边界写进制度和合同里？这些答案，比“模型又提升了多少分”更能解释下一笔预算会流向哪里。

points: 过去一年，AI 编程工具的卖点几乎都围绕“它更会写”。但在企业里，真正让人犹豫的往往不是它能不能写出一段像样的代码，而是下一步：它能不能被允许去读完整的代码库，能不能去调用内部工具，能不能触碰数据库，能不能在出错后留下足够清楚的记录，能不能在被误用时立刻停下来。

calibration.title: Aaron Levie｜Right now there’s a temporary mismatch between the jobs that used to be sought after in so
calibration.originalView: Right now there’s a temporary mismatch between the jobs that used to be sought after in some fields and the new jobs that are becoming in demand in those fields. For instance, if you studied CS, for years the general direction of travel was often to join a tech company and build customer-facing software in some form. A significant portion of the CS pipeline from college to hire was built for this. When you realize that AI is going to make coding abundant, you realize everyone will need technical talent to implement agentic systems. This means the types of roles engineers should be thinking about radically expands. I was talking to a Fortune 500 pharma CEO a week ago that commented on how much more technical talent they need right now. The job may be different from what it was 5 years ago when thinking about tech, but the demand for the skills are still there. And this is what I’m hearing from every CIO and CEO across nearly every industry right now. We definitely need colleges to wake up to this; but we equally need companies think about how they craft pipelines into these jobs. ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI likes=275; retweets=24; replies=37
